Data Visualization for Exploratory Data Analysis

Data Visualization Project 03

In this exercise you will explore methods to create different types of data visualizations (such as plotting text data, or exploring the distributions of continuous variables).

PART 1: Density Plots

Using the dataset obtained from FSU’s Florida Climate Center, for a station at Tampa International Airport (TPA) for 2022, attempt to recreate the charts shown below which were generated using data from 2016. You can read the 2022 dataset using the code below:

library(tidyverse)
library(tidytext)
library(wordcloud)
weather_tpa <- read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aalhamadani/datasets/master/tpa_weather_2022.csv")
# random sample 
sample_n(weather_tpa, 4)
## # A tibble: 4 × 7
##    year month   day precipitation max_temp min_temp ave_temp
##   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>         <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
## 1  2022     8    13          0          92       80     86  
## 2  2022     7    23          1.69       94       77     85.5
## 3  2022     4    15          0.02       87       73     80  
## 4  2022     2    27          0          82       69     75.5

See Slides from Week 4 of Visualizing Relationships and Models (slide 10) for a reminder on how to use this type of dataset with the lubridate package for dates and times (example included in the slides uses data from 2016).

Using the 2022 data:

  1. Create a plot like the one below:

Hint: the option binwidth = 3 was used with the geom_histogram() function.

weather_tpa<- weather_tpa %>%
  mutate(
    date = make_date(year, month, day),
    month_name = month(date, label = TRUE, abbr = FALSE)
  )
ggplot(weather_tpa, aes(x = max_temp, fill = month_name)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 3, color = "white", linewidth = 0.1, show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ month_name, ncol = 4) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d() +
  labs(
    x = "Maximum temperatures",
    y = "Number of Days"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "grey", color = NA),
    strip.text = element_text(color = "black", face = "plain"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

  1. Create a plot like the one below:

Hint: check the kernel parameter of the geom_density() function, and use bw = 0.5.

weather_tpa <- weather_tpa %>%
  mutate(
    date = make_date(year, month, day),
    month_name = month(date, label = TRUE, abbr = FALSE)
  )
weather_tpa %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = max_temp)) +
  geom_area(stat = "density", fill = "gray", color = "black") +
  labs(x = "Maximum temperature", y = "density") +
  theme_bw()

  1. Create a plot like the one below:

Hint: default options for geom_density() were used.

weather_tpa <- weather_tpa %>%
  mutate(
    date = make_date(year, month, day),
    month_name = month(date, label = TRUE, abbr = FALSE)
  )
weather_tpa %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = max_temp, fill = month_name)) +
  geom_density(color = "black", show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ month_name, ncol = 4) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d() +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 0.25, by = 0.05), limits = c(0, 0.25)) +
  labs(
    title = "Density plots for each month in 2022",
    x = "Maximum temperatures",
    y = "density"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    strip.background = element_rect(fill = "grey", color = NA),
    strip.text = element_text(color = "black", face = "plain"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
## Warning: Removed 10 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
## (`geom_density()`).

  1. Generate a plot like the chart below:

Hint: use the{ggridges} package, and the geom_density_ridges() function paying close attention to the quantile_lines and quantiles parameters. The plot above uses the plasma option (color scale) for the viridis palette.

weather_tpa <- weather_tpa %>%
  mutate(
    date = make_date(year, month, day),
    month_name = month(date, label = TRUE, abbr = FALSE)
  )
month_levels <- levels(weather_tpa$month_name)
overlap <- 1.5
density_df <- weather_tpa %>%
  group_by(month_name) %>%
  group_modify(~ {
    d <- density(.x$max_temp, n = 512)
    tibble(x = d$x, y = d$y)
  }) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(
    month_index = as.numeric(factor(month_name, levels = month_levels)),
    y_offset = month_index * overlap,
    y_top = y_offset + y
  )
baseline_df <- density_df %>%
  distinct(month_name, month_index, y_offset)
ggplot(density_df, aes(x = x, group = month_name)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = y_offset, ymax = y_top, fill = x)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = y_top), color = "black", linewidth = 0.4) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma", name = "") +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = baseline_df$y_offset,
    labels = baseline_df$month_name
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Maximum temperature in Fahrenheit",
    y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 12)
  )

  1. Create a plot of your choice that uses the attribute for precipitation (values of -99.9 for temperature or -99.99 for precipitation represent missing data).
weather_tpa <- weather_tpa %>%
  filter(precipitation != -99.99) %>%
  mutate(date = make_date(year, month, day))

ggplot(weather_tpa, aes(x = date, y = precipitation)) +
  geom_point(color = "lightblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Daily Precipitation in Tampa Florida",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Precipitation in inches"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

PART 2

You can choose to work on either Option (A) or Option (B). Remove from this template the option you decided not to work on.

Option (A): Visualizing Text Data

Review the set of slides (and additional resources linked in it) for visualizing text data: Week 6 PowerPoint slides of Visualizing Text Data.

Choose any dataset with text data, and create at least one visualization with it. For example, you can create a frequency count of most used bigrams, a sentiment analysis of the text data, a network visualization of terms commonly used together, and/or a visualization of a topic modeling approach to the problem of identifying words/documents associated to different topics in the text data you decide to use.

Make sure to include a copy of the dataset in the data/ folder, and reference your sources if different from the ones listed below:

  • Billboard Top 100 Lyrics

  • RateMyProfessors comments

  • FL Poly News Articles

(to get the “raw” data from any of the links listed above, simply click on the raw button of the GitHub page and copy the URL to be able to read it in your computer using the read_csv() function)

lyrics <- read_csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aalhamadani/dataviz_final_project/main/data/BB_top100_2015.csv") %>%
  filter(!is.na(Lyrics))
## Rows: 100 Columns: 6
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr (3): Song, Artist, Lyrics
## dbl (3): Rank, Year, Source
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
word_counts <- lyrics %>%
  unnest_tokens(word, Lyrics) %>%
  anti_join(stop_words, by = "word") %>%
  count(word, sort = TRUE)
set.seed(1)
wordcloud(
  words = word_counts$word,
  freq = word_counts$n,
  max.words = 100,
  random.order = FALSE,
  colors = "purple"
)
